assumptions: Commercial reasoning is directional until user interviews, traffic, or payment intent are measured.
Architecture choices must stay aligned with the accepted creative direction.

directions.audience: a narrow first user group with an urgent, repeated workflow
directions.name: Focused MVP
directions.promise: Ship the smallest useful version of: Decision dashboard with a TypeScript frontend and Python REST API backend
directions.reason: fastest path to validation and lowest scope risk
directions.target_user_pain: The user needs one painful job completed reliably before broader platform value matters.

directions.audience: users who know the outcome they want but not the sequence of choices
directions.name: Guided Workflow
directions.promise: Turn the problem into a step-by-step decision journey.
directions.reason: best fit when the idea is vague and users need momentum more than customization
directions.target_user_pain: The user is blocked by ambiguity, not lack of tools.

directions.audience: teams that need shared confidence before implementation
directions.name: Evidence Dashboard
directions.promise: Make options, evidence, confidence, and next actions visible.
directions.reason: commercially strongest when stakeholders must compare options and justify tradeoffs
directions.target_user_pain: The team needs decisions to be inspectable, repeatable, and defensible.

creative_decision.goal: Decision dashboard with a TypeScript frontend and Python REST API backend
creative_decision.next_decision: Confirm the selected direction before architecture is treated as accepted.

selected_direction.audience: teams that need shared confidence before implementation
selected_direction.name: Evidence Dashboard
selected_direction.promise: Make options, evidence, confidence, and next actions visible.
selected_direction.reason: commercially strongest when stakeholders must compare options and justify tradeoffs
selected_direction.target_user_pain: The team needs decisions to be inspectable, repeatable, and defensible.

evidence.summary: Next.js supports TypeScript web applications and dashboard-style product surfaces.
evidence.title: Next.js documentation

evidence.summary: FastAPI supports Python REST APIs with OpenAPI contracts for separated backend services.
evidence.title: FastAPI documentation

warnings: No application entrypoint was detected statically.

project.goal: Decision dashboard with a TypeScript frontend and Python REST API backend

risks.description: Deep coupling with Vercel platform may increase migration cost if self-hosting becomes required.
risks.mitigation: Use standard Next.js build output; avoid Vercel-specific APIs (ISR with file system, Edge Config) unless explicitly accepted.
risks.signal_trigger: Self-hosting or on-premise deployment requirement emerges.

risks.description: Hybrid SSR/SSG/ISR rendering model can cause confusing caching and hydration behavior.
risks.mitigation: Default to SSG for content pages, SSR for authenticated dashboards only. Document the rendering strategy per route.
risks.signal_trigger: Performance regressions or hydration mismatch errors in production logs.

risks.description: Mixing sync and async handlers can block the event loop under load.
risks.mitigation: Audit all route handlers; mark sync-only routes explicitly; run load tests before launch.
risks.signal_trigger: P99 latency spikes under concurrent request load.

risks.description: FastAPI does not enforce CSRF or rate-limiting by default; these must be configured manually.
risks.mitigation: Add slowapi for rate-limiting and a CSRF middleware before the first production deployment.
risks.signal_trigger: Security audit identifies missing rate limiting.

risks.description: Project scope may expand beyond the original creative direction, invalidating current architecture assumptions.
risks.mitigation: Run project-forge revise whenever a significant new feature or constraint is introduced.
risks.signal_trigger: A new feature, stakeholder, or constraint is introduced that was not in the original ADR context.

acceptance_criteria: Preserve the accepted ADR unless new evidence is recorded.
Run the relevant structured harness commands and explain remaining failures.
Keep implementation scope inside the accepted creative direction.
Return to Project Forge when direction, architecture, or harness assumptions become stale.
superpowers.assignment: Consume this packet and implement without re-litigating accepted product and architecture decisions.
consume_steps: Read this packet and the Markdown handoff.
Open the ADR, creative decision, inventory, and evidence only when more detail is needed.
Use Superpowers implementation workflows after accepting the packet.
superpowers.first_task: Implement the smallest user-visible workflow from the accepted creative direction and prove it with the harness contract.
guardrails: Project Forge does not replace TDD, debugging, code review, worktrees, or branch completion.
Do not silently replace provisional evidence with unsupported claims.
Do not execute commands that are absent from the harness contract.
